transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Charlie. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, thanks for having me. I guess I’ve been thinking a lot about, well, relationships and how they seem to go nowhere sometimes. Like, I’ve reached out to someone I like, and I just... I don’t know, I feel like I’m stuck in this loop of waiting for a response that never comes. It’s kind of frustrating.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling frustrated and maybe a bit stuck with this situation in your relationships, especially with someone you like. Waiting for a response can be really challenging. Can you tell me more about what thoughts or feelings come up for you while you’re waiting?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... when I don’t hear back, I start thinking maybe they’re not interested, or maybe I messed something up. It makes me feel really down, like I’m just waiting for something that’s not going to happen. I guess I end up ruminating on it a lot, which doesn’t help. It’s like I can’t stop thinking about it, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the uncertainty of not hearing back leads you to question their interest and your own actions, which then brings you down and keeps you stuck in those thoughts. That cycle of rumination can be really tough to break. When you find yourself caught in those thoughts, how do you usually cope or respond to those feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I don’t know, I guess I just try to distract myself sometimes, but it’s hard. I might watch something or scroll through my phone, but it doesn’t really change how I feel. I just keep coming back to the same thoughts. Sometimes, I talk to friends about it, but I don’t want to burden them with my worries. So, I end up keeping a lot of it to myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re trying to find ways to distract yourself, but those distractions don’t really address the underlying feelings. It’s understandable that you might hesitate to share your worries with friends, wanting to avoid feeling like a burden. Keeping those feelings inside can be really heavy. How do you feel when you do talk to your friends about it?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: When I do talk to them, it’s a bit of a relief, I guess. But I still feel like I’m holding back a lot. I worry that they won’t really understand or that they’ll think I’m overreacting. So, I try to keep it light, but sometimes I wish I could just be more open about what I’m really feeling. It’s just... hard to find the right words, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that you’d feel a sense of relief when you share with your friends, even if you’re holding back some of your deeper feelings. It sounds like there’s a desire to be more open, but also a fear of not being understood or being seen as overreacting. Finding the right words can be really challenging, especially when emotions are involved. What do you think holds you back from expressing those deeper feelings when you’re with them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think part of it is just this fear of judgment. I don’t want them to think I’m being dramatic or that I’m too invested in something that might not even matter. It feels safer to keep things light and not dive too deep. Plus, I guess I’m not always sure if they’d really get it. It’s like I don’t want to make things awkward or uncomfortable. So, I just... hold back.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the fear of judgment and the desire to avoid awkwardness play a big role in why you hold back. Keeping things light feels like a safer option, even if it means not fully expressing what you’re going through. It’s understandable to want to protect yourself and your relationships in that way. When you think about those deeper feelings, what do you wish you could share with them if you felt safe to do so?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I wish I could share how much I really care about this person and how the waiting makes me feel anxious and insecure. I want them to know that it’s not just about the lack of response; it’s about how it affects me emotionally. I’d like to express my fears of being rejected or not being good enough. But then I worry it might come off as too intense or needy, and I don’t want to scare anyone away. So, I just keep it to myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there’s a lot of depth to your feelings that you wish you could express—your care for this person, the anxiety and insecurity that come with the waiting, and your fears of rejection. Those are really vulnerable emotions to share, and it makes sense that you’d be concerned about how it might be received. Balancing the desire to be open with the fear of coming on too strong can be really tough. How do you feel about the idea of finding a way to express some of those feelings, even if it’s just a little bit?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it would be nice to find a way to express some of those feelings, but I’m not sure how to start. It feels risky, you know? I worry about how they might react, and what if it doesn’t go well? But at the same time, I feel like holding it all in is just making things harder for me. Maybe if I could find a way to share just a bit, it could help me feel less alone in it. It’s just figuring out how to do that without feeling too exposed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel that way—wanting to express yourself while also feeling the weight of the risk involved. It sounds like you’re recognizing that sharing even a little could help alleviate some of that loneliness, but the fear of being too exposed is holding you back. Finding a balance between vulnerability and safety can be tricky. What do you think might help you feel more comfortable taking that first step, even if it’s a small one?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe starting with something less intense could help, like just mentioning that I’ve been feeling a bit anxious about the situation without going into all the details. That way, it’s not too overwhelming for either of us. I think if I could gauge their reaction, it might give me the confidence to share more if it feels right. It’s just... figuring out how to bring it up without it feeling forced. But I guess that could be a start.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach—starting with something less intense could create a space for you to share without feeling overwhelmed. By gauging their reaction, you might find the confidence to open up more if it feels right. It’s great that you’re considering how to bring it up in a natural way. What do you think might help you feel more at ease when you decide to share that initial feeling?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it might help to remind myself that it’s okay to feel anxious about this and that my feelings are valid. Maybe even practicing what I want to say beforehand could make it feel less daunting. I could also try to focus on the idea that my friends care about me and would likely want to support me, even if I’m a bit nervous. Just having that mindset might help me feel more at ease when the moment comes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are really helpful strategies—reminding yourself that your feelings are valid and practicing what you want to say can definitely ease some of the anxiety. Focusing on the support and care your friends have for you is a positive mindset to cultivate. It sounds like you’re building a solid foundation for when you do decide to share. How do you feel about taking those steps, and is there anything else you think might support you in this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful thinking about it that way. It’s nice to think that I could actually share something and maybe feel a little lighter afterward. I guess having a plan in mind makes it seem more manageable. It might also help to remind myself that it’s okay if the conversation doesn’t go perfectly. Just taking that step could be a win in itself. I’m not sure what else I might need, but maybe just keeping that supportive mindset will be enough for now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful and that having a plan makes it seem more manageable. Recognizing that taking that step is a win in itself, regardless of how the conversation goes, is such a healthy perspective. It sounds like you’re cultivating a supportive mindset that will help you navigate this. If you ever feel the need to revisit this or explore more about your feelings, I’m here to support you. Is there anything else on your mind today, or do you feel ready to take a break for now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I appreciate that, thank you. I think I’m feeling a bit more settled now, and it’s nice to have talked through some of this. I don’t have anything else pressing on my mind right now, so I think a break sounds good. I’ll definitely keep in mind what we discussed about sharing my feelings. Thanks for listening and helping me sort through it all.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you’re feeling more settled and that our conversation has been helpful for you. It’s great that you’re considering sharing your feelings, and I hope it brings you some relief. Remember, it’s okay to take things at your own pace. If you ever want to talk again, I’m here for you. Take care, Charlie, and enjoy your break!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. I’ll definitely keep that in mind. It’s nice to know I have someone to talk to if I need it. Take care, and I’ll see you next time!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Charlie. I’m glad you feel that support is there for you. Take care of yourself, and I look forward to our next conversation. See you next time!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: See you next time! Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Take care, Charlie! Looking forward to chatting again. See you next time!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks! I’m looking forward to it too. See you next time!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're welcome! See you next time, Charlie! Take care!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks! You too! See you next time!
